questions.text: Which is the largest desert in the world?
questions.explanation: The Antarctic Desert (~14 million km²) is the largest desert in the world. A desert is defined by aridity (low precipitation), not temperature. The Sahara (~9.2 million km²) is the largest hot desert but not the largest overall.

questions.text: The world's largest tropical rainforest is:
questions.explanation: The Amazon Rainforest (~5.5 million km²) in South America is the world's largest tropical rainforest. About 60% is in Brazil, with the rest in Peru, Colombia, and other South American countries. Congo is the world's 2nd largest tropical rainforest (in Africa).

questions.text: The 'Sundarbans' mangrove forest is located in:
questions.explanation: The Sundarbans mangrove forest spans both India (West Bengal) and Bangladesh, at the Ganges-Brahmaputra delta. It is the world's largest mangrove forest (~10,000 km²) and a UNESCO World Heritage Site hosting the Royal Bengal Tiger.

questions.text: What soil type is found in temperate grasslands like the Steppes and Prairies?
questions.explanation: Chernozem (black earth) is the rich, dark soil found in temperate grasslands — the Steppes of Russia/Ukraine, Prairies of North America, and Pampas of South America. It is humus-rich and the world's most fertile natural soil.

questions.text: Which desert is the driest non-polar desert in the world?
questions.explanation: The Atacama Desert in Chile/Peru is the driest non-polar desert on Earth. Some meteorological stations there have never recorded any rainfall. It is a cold coastal desert cooled by the Humboldt (Peru) Current flowing along the South American coast.

questions.text: Taiga forests are dominated by which type of trees?
questions.explanation: Taiga (boreal forest) is dominated by conifers — spruce, fir, pine, and larch. These needle-leaved trees are adapted to cold, long winters with heavy snow. Exception: larch is a conifer but drops its needles (deciduous conifer).

questions.text: The Kalahari Desert is best described as:
options: A true hot desert with less than 250mm rainfall
A cold desert like the Gobi
A semi-arid grassland receiving more rainfall than true deserts
questions.explanation: The Kalahari is technically semi-arid, receiving 150–500 mm of rainfall annually — more than true deserts. It supports grasses, shrubs, and wildlife (meerkats, gemsbok). It is sometimes called a 'fossil desert' as it was drier in the past.

questions.text: The 'Rub' al Khali' (Empty Quarter) is part of which desert?
questions.explanation: The Rub' al Khali ('Empty Quarter') is the world's largest continuous sand desert, located in the southeastern Arabian Peninsula within the Arabian Desert. It covers about 650,000 km² across Saudi Arabia, UAE, Oman, and Yemen.

questions.text: What percentage of global oxygen does the Amazon rainforest produce approximately?
questions.explanation: The Amazon rainforest is often said to produce approximately 20% of Earth's oxygen — earning it the nickname 'Lungs of the Earth.' However, it also consumes much of that oxygen through decomposition; net production for the atmosphere is more complex.

questions.text: The Gobi Desert is classified as which type of desert?
questions.explanation: The Gobi Desert (China and Mongolia) is a cold desert — it has hot summers but very cold winters (can drop to −40°C). It is the largest desert in Asia and the 5th largest in the world. Unlike the Sahara, it is not predominantly sandy — most is rocky.

questions.text: The 'Serengeti National Park' (famous for wildebeest migration) is in which country?
questions.explanation: Serengeti National Park is in Tanzania. The annual wildebeest migration moves between Serengeti (Tanzania) and Masai Mara (Kenya), crossing the Mara River. Masai Mara is Kenya's reserve; Serengeti is Tanzania's national park.

questions.text: Shifting cultivation is called 'Jhum' in India. In which region of India is it primarily practiced?
questions.explanation: Jhum cultivation (slash-and-burn farming) is primarily practiced in Northeast India (Nagaland, Mizoram, Meghalaya, Arunachal Pradesh, Manipur). Farmers clear forest, farm for 2–3 years, then move on, allowing the land to regenerate.

questions.text: Which grassland region is known as the 'Pampas'?
questions.explanation: Pampas are the temperate grasslands of South America — primarily Argentina (also Uruguay and southern Brazil). They are known for cattle ranching and wheat farming. Argentina is among the world's largest beef exporters, thanks largely to Pampas grasslands.

questions.text: The Thar Desert (Great Indian Desert) is located primarily in which Indian state?
questions.explanation: The Thar Desert is located primarily in Rajasthan (India) and extends into Sind and Punjab provinces of Pakistan. It is India's only major desert and receives some SW monsoon rainfall — making it less arid than the Sahara.

questions.text: The Amazon rainforest is often called the 'lungs of the Earth' because it:
options: Stores all carbon for the planet
questions.explanation: The Amazon rainforest is called 'lungs of the Earth' primarily because it produces approximately 20% of Earth's oxygen through photosynthesis. This vast forest also absorbs enormous amounts of CO₂, making it critical for climate regulation.

questions.text: Mangrove forests are found in which type of coastal environment?
questions.explanation: Mangroves grow in tropical and subtropical intertidal zones — coastal areas regularly submerged and exposed with tidal cycles. They need warm temperatures, saline to brackish water, and tidal flow. They protect coastlines from waves, tsunamis, and erosion.
